segment.source: No secrets found. Click "Create Secret" to add one.
Hash chain is valid - no tampering detected
Manage encrypted secrets stored in the vault.
View access logs and verify audit chain integrity.
Detect and migrate plaintext secrets to the vault.
Successfully migrated %d secret(s) to the vault.
Secure storage and management for sensitive configuration data.
No master key provider is configured or accessible. Vault cannot encrypt or decrypt secrets.
Ensure that %s is set, or configure an alternative master key provider (environment variable or file-based).
Master key provider "%s" is available but encryption is broken.
Encryption is active using the %s master key provider.
Navigate to Secrets and click the + button in the header to create a new secret. Provide an identifier (e.g., stripe_api_key) and the secret value.
Reference secrets in your TYPO3 configuration using the vault syntax:
Payment gateways, external services, or any secret that differs per site. Resolved at runtime from config/sites/*/config.yaml.
Let editors manage secrets per record (e.g., per-integration or per-client settings). Values encrypted automatically via TCA.
When your extension needs secrets programmatically - API clients, payment processors, external integrations.
PSR-18 compatible API calls with automatic secret injection. Supports Bearer, API-Key, Basic Auth, OAuth2, and more.
Automate secret provisioning in deployment pipelines, or manage secrets via scripts without backend access.
When secrets must appear in rendered output (e.g., JS API keys). Requires frontend_accessible flag and cache disabled.
Shorthand for common vault field patterns with sensible defaults (exclude: true, l10n_mode: exclude).
Initialize vault and generate master key
Scan for plaintext secrets in database
Migrate a database field to vault
Run bin/typo3 vault:<command> --help for detailed usage.
Each secret is encrypted with its own unique Data Encryption Key (DEK), which is then encrypted with the master key.
Secrets can be restricted to specific backend users or groups. Every access is logged in the audit trail.
Tamper-evident audit chain records all secret operations with cryptographic integrity verification.
This guide explains how vault encryption works, how to manage secrets, and how to troubleshoot common issues.
The Vault extension provides secure storage for sensitive configuration data (API keys, passwords, tokens) using envelope encryption. Follow these steps to get started:

Visit the Dashboard and check the health indicator at the top. A green banner confirms that encryption is active and working. If you see an error, ensure that %s is set in your TYPO3 configuration or configure an alternative master key provider.
Navigate to Secrets and click the + button in the header. Choose a descriptive identifier (e.g., stripe_api_key) and enter the secret value. The value is encrypted before it reaches the database.
Use the vault syntax anywhere TYPO3 resolves configuration values:
This works in site configuration YAML, TypoScript, and any configuration that uses the TYPO3 expression language.
The Migration Wizard scans your database for plaintext secrets and guides you through encrypting them. It supports batch processing and verifies each migration before committing.
Vault uses a two-layer encryption scheme called envelope encryption, the same pattern used by AWS KMS, Google Cloud KMS, and Azure Key Vault:
Each secret is encrypted with its own unique random key using AES-256-GCM. This means compromising one secret does not reveal others.
The DEK is then encrypted with the master key and stored alongside the ciphertext. The master key never leaves the server.
The master key can come from several sources (in priority order):
a key file outside the web root
The master key can be rotated without downtime using %s. This re-encrypts all DEKs with the new master key. Individual secrets can also be rotated with %s.
Every secret operation (create, read, update, delete, rotate) is recorded in a tamper-evident audit chain. Each entry includes:
The action performed and the secret identifier
The backend user who performed the action
A cryptographic hash linking each entry to the previous one
Use the Audit Log module to browse entries and the Verify Chain action to check integrity. Any tampered or missing entries will be flagged.
"Encryption is not available" on the dashboard
The master key provider could not be initialized. Check that at least one of the following is configured:
The TYPO3_VAULT_MASTER_KEY environment variable is set
A valid key file path is configured in extension settings
The TYPO3 encryptionKey is not empty

This means the master key has changed since the secret was encrypted. If the master key was rotated correctly, all DEKs should have been re-encrypted. If the key was lost, the affected secrets cannot be recovered — store a new value.

The scanner looks for fields matching common secret patterns (columns named *password*, *key*, *token*, *secret*). If your field uses a different naming convention, you can add it manually via the CLI:

A broken chain means an entry was modified or deleted outside of vault operations. This could indicate tampering or a database restore from an inconsistent backup. The verification report shows exactly which entries are affected.
Can I use vault secrets in site configuration YAML?
Yes. Use the %vault(identifier)% syntax in your config/sites/*/config.yaml. Values are resolved at runtime, so they never appear in plain text in files.
What happens if I lose the master key?

Without the master key, encrypted secrets cannot be recovered. This is by design — the security of envelope encryption depends on the master key. Always back up your master key separately from the database.

The vault backend module is restricted to administrators. However, editors can interact with vault-backed TCA fields (e.g., renderType=vaultSecret) without seeing the actual values — they enter values which are encrypted automatically.
How do I rotate secrets in a CI/CD pipeline?
Use the CLI commands in your deployment scripts:
Ensure the TYPO3_VAULT_MASTER_KEY environment variable is available in your CI/CD environment.
This wizard helps you detect and migrate plaintext secrets from your database and configuration to the secure vault.
The system scans your database columns and extension configuration for values that look like secrets (API keys, passwords, tokens).
You review the detected secrets and select which ones to migrate.
Set migration options like identifier patterns and ownership.
The selected secrets are encrypted and stored in the vault, and database columns are updated with vault references.
Confirm that the migration completed successfully.
Always create a database backup before running migrations.
Run the scan on a test system first to review what will be detected.
Not all detected values are actual secrets. Review carefully before migrating.
Hashed passwords (bcrypt, argon2) are automatically excluded as they are already secure.
Configuration secrets (in LocalConfiguration.php or extension settings) are detected but must be migrated manually. Replace the value with %vault(identifier)% after storing the secret in the vault.
The system has scanned your TYPO3 installation for potential plaintext secrets that should be migrated to the vault.
Known API key formats (Stripe, AWS, GitHub, etc.). "—" means detected by field name only.
Database records affected. "n/a" for configuration secrets (must be migrated manually).
Only configuration secrets were detected. These must be migrated manually by updating your configuration to use %vault(identifier)% references. See the documentation for details.
Great! No potential plaintext secrets were found in your database or configuration. Your sensitive data appears to be properly protected.
Select which database secrets you want to migrate to the vault. Configuration secrets must be migrated manually.
Only database secrets can be migrated via this wizard. Configuration secrets need to be updated manually to use %vault(identifier)% references.
Known API key formats (Stripe, AWS, GitHub, etc.). "—" means detected by field name only.
No database secrets were found to migrate. The scan may have detected only configuration secrets, which must be migrated manually by updating your configuration to use %vault(identifier)% references.
Review the migration settings for each selected secret. The identifier pattern determines how secrets will be stored in the vault.
This will modify your database. Make sure you have a backup before proceeding.
Use {uid} as placeholder for record UID
Replace original values with vault identifiers
When enabled, the original plaintext values in the database will be replaced with vault identifiers. This is the recommended approach for production environments.
Original plaintext values have been replaced with vault identifiers in the database.
Update your extension code to use VaultFieldResolver::resolveFields() to retrieve secrets
Test that your application still works correctly with the migrated secrets
Consider running another scan to check for any remaining plaintext secrets
Thank you from Kurt Dirnbauer. © NetResearch DTT GmbH
List of stored secrets with their metadata and management actions
The audit log hash chain has been verified successfully. No tampering or modifications have been detected.
Hash chain verification failed. The audit log may have been tampered with.
